--- title: "[AFT]{.doc-object-name .doc-class .doc-label .doc-label-class}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} Parametric accelerated failure time model. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_parametric.py#L169-L821){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.CLASS} ```python AFT(dist="weibull", *, conf_level=0.95) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} While the Cox proportional hazards model leaves the baseline hazard unspecified, AFT models assume a fully parametric distribution for survival times and model how covariates accelerate or decelerate the "clock" of failure. Specifically, $\log(T) = \mu + \beta^\top x + \sigma\varepsilon$, where $T$ is survival time, $\beta$ are log-time-scale coefficients, $\sigma$ is a scale parameter, and $\varepsilon$ follows a specified error distribution (e.g., extreme-value, logistic, normal). This means a unit increase in covariate $x$ multiplies survival time by $\exp(\beta)$. AFT models are useful when you want explicit, interpretable survival time predictions or when the parametric assumptions are reasonable. Unlike Cox models, they require choosing a distributional family (Weibull, exponential, lognormal, or loglogistic). Call `fit()` with a right-censored `Surv` response and a design matrix. The model automatically adds an intercept and estimates coefficients (on the log-time scale), the scale parameter, and standard errors via maximum likelihood. The implementation uses numerical optimization (typically Newton-Raphson) to maximize the likelihood. Coefficients on the log-time scale can be exponentiated to obtain time- acceleration ratios: $\exp(\beta)$ is the multiplicative effect on median or mean survival. The model also supports prediction of survival probabilities and quantiles at future times given covariate values. ::: ## Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [dist]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [["weibull"]{.st}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Error distribution: `"weibull"` (default), `"exponential"`, `"lognormal"`, or `"loglogistic"`. [conf_level]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.95]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Confidence level for coefficient intervals (default is `0.95`). ::: ## Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Fitted estimator]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Call `fit()` to produce a fitted estimator with cached results (`coef_`, `scale_`, `std_error_`, `z_`, `p_value_`, `conf_low_`, `conf_high_`, `loglik_`, `aic_`, `bic_`), accessible as arrays or exported to DataFrames. ::: ## Details {.doc-details} Call `fit(surv, covariates)` with a right-censored `Surv` response and a covariate design (a 2-D array or a dataframe). An intercept is added automatically; rows with missing covariates are dropped. Results are exposed as arrays (`coef_`, `scale_`, `std_error_`, `z_`, `p_value_`) and as tidy frames via `to_frame()` (optionally `format=`) and `greenwood.tidy`. ## Examples {.doc-examples} Build a `Surv` response from the bundled `lung` dataset and fit a Weibull AFT model with `age` and `sex` as covariates. Printing the fitted object reports the coefficients (on the log-time scale), the scale, and the log-likelihood. ::: {#a28cb609 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) aft = gw.AFT("weibull").fit(y, lung[["age", "sex"]]) aft ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` AFT (accelerated failure time model, dist='weibull') coef se(coef) z p (Intercept) 6.275 0.4814 13.036 7.671e-39 age -0.01226 0.006957 -1.762 0.07811 sex 0.3821 0.1275 2.997 0.002723 Scale = 0.7541 n = 228, events = 165 Log-likelihood = -1147 ``` ::: ::: ## Methods {.doc-methods}
Name Description
fit() Fit the accelerated failure time model to survival data.
predict() Predict survival times, quantiles, or survival probabilities from the AFT model.
to_frame() Return the coefficient table as a DataFrame.
### [fit()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Fit the accelerated failure time model to survival data. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_parametric.py#L262-L382){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python fit(surv, covariates, *, data=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Fits a parametric accelerated failure time (AFT) model to a right-censored response and covariates. The AFT models the log-survival time as a linear regression on covariates plus a random error from a specified parametric distribution (Weibull, exponential, log-normal, or log-logistic). An intercept is added automatically. The AFT is a parametric alternative to Cox regression, providing a fully specified survival distribution at the cost of stronger distributional assumptions. Unlike Cox, AFT supports median survival predictions and is naturally interpreted on the log-time scale: a coefficient of 0.1 means the covariate multiplies survival time by $\exp(0.1)$. Results are stored in the fitted object as coefficient arrays and can be exported to DataFrames. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [surv]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A right-censored `Surv` response. Built with `Surv.right()`. Interval-censored or other response types raise `NotImplementedError`. [covariates]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A dataframe (pandas or polars), a 2-D array, or a formula string (e.g., `"age + sex"`) evaluated against the `data` argument. [data]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : A dataframe to evaluate the formula string (ignored if `covariates` is a dataframe or array). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [AFT]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The fitted estimator object itself (for method chaining) with cached coefficient arrays (`coef_`, `std_error_`, `z_`, `p_value_`), scale parameter (`scale_`), and log-likelihood (`loglik_`). ::: #### Details {.doc-details} The AFT model parameterizes log-survival time as $\log(T) = X\beta + \sigma\varepsilon$, where $X$ is the design matrix, $\beta$ are coefficients, $\sigma$ is a scale parameter, and $\varepsilon$ is an error term from the chosen distribution. The survival function is then $S(t \mid X) = P(T > t \mid X) = G((\log(t) - X\beta) / \sigma)$, where $G$ is the survival function of the error distribution. Estimation uses maximum likelihood via numerical optimization. Exponential and Weibull models are nested special cases; log-normal and log-logistic offer different tail behaviors. #### Examples {.doc-examples} Fit a log-normal AFT model on the bundled `lung` dataset with `age` and `sex` as covariates: ::: {#8aa091b2 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) aft = gw.AFT(dist="lognormal").fit(y, lung[["age", "sex"]]) aft ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` AFT (accelerated failure time model, dist='lognormal') coef se(coef) z p (Intercept) 6.408 0.5929 10.807 3.186e-27 age -0.02336 0.008389 -2.784 0.005363 sex 0.5193 0.1552 3.347 0.0008176 Scale = 1.053 n = 228, events = 165 Log-likelihood = -1159 ``` ::: ::: Use a formula string with the `data` argument: ::: {#3dd69ae7 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft_formula = gw.AFT(dist="weibull").fit(y, "age + sex", data=lung) aft_formula ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` AFT (accelerated failure time model, dist='weibull') coef se(coef) z p (Intercept) 6.275 0.4814 13.036 7.671e-39 age -0.01226 0.006957 -1.762 0.07811 sex 0.3821 0.1275 2.997 0.002723 Scale = 0.7541 n = 228, events = 165 Log-likelihood = -1147 ``` ::: ::: ### [predict()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Predict survival times, quantiles, or survival probabilities from the AFT model. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_parametric.py#L454-L763){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python predict( newdata=None, *, type="survival", times=None, p=0.5, tau=None, conditional_after=None, ci=False, conf_type="log-log", format=None ) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Generates predictions from a fitted accelerated failure time model. The AFT is a fully parametric survival model, so predictions require specifying both the predictor values (via `newdata`) and the type of prediction desired. Pass `newdata=None` to predict for the training data (fitted subjects). Six prediction types are available: 1. **Linear predictor** (`type="lp"`): the log-time location $X\beta$, showing how covariates shift the log-survival time distribution. 2. **Quantile** (`type="quantile"`): predicted survival-time quantiles at specified failure probabilities (e.g., median survival when $p=0.5$). Useful for clinical summaries like "50% of subjects with these covariates survive to time X." 3. **Survival** (`type="survival"`): survival probabilities $S(t \mid x)$ at specified times, returned as a DataFrame for easy visualization. Optionally condition on already having survived to a landmark time (`conditional_after`) for landmark-based predictions. With `ci=True`, confidence intervals are included. 4. **Mean** (`type="mean"`): the expected survival time $E[T]$ (unconditional) or the conditional mean $E[T \mid T > t_0]$ when `conditional_after` is provided. Computed via closed-form formulas for all distributions. Returns an array of shape (n_subjects,). 5. **Mean remaining** (`type="mean_remaining"`): expected remaining lifetime $E[T - t_0 \mid T > t_0]$ for subjects known to have survived past `t0`. Requires `conditional_after`. Returns an array of shape (n_subjects,). 6. **RMST** (`type="rmst"`): restricted mean survival time $E[\min(T, \tau)]$ up to the restriction time `tau`. Requires the `tau` argument. Returns an array of shape (n_subjects,). ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [newdata]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Covariate values for prediction. A DataFrame (Pandas or Polars), 2-D array, or `None` (default). If `None`, uses the training data (design matrix used at fit time). Must have the same columns/features as the training data. [type]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [["survival"]{.st}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Prediction type (default `"survival"`): - `"lp"`: Linear predictor $X\beta$ (log-time location). Returns an array. - `"quantile"`: Survival-time quantiles at failure probabilities `p`. Returns a frame with `p` column and one column per subject. - `"survival"`: Survival probabilities $S(t \mid x)$ at times in `times`. Returns a frame with `time` column and one column per subject (one per query time, one per subject in newdata). [times]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Query times for `type="survival"` (ignored for other types). An array-like of floats. If None (default), uses an automatic grid based on the fitted distribution (50 equally spaced times on the log scale, rounded). [p]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.5]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Failure probabilities for `type="quantile"` (ignored for other types). Can be a scalar (e.g., `0.5` for median) or array-like. Default `0.5` (median). Must be in (0, 1). [tau]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Restriction time for `type="rmst"`. Scalar float giving the upper limit of integration. Required when `type="rmst"`, ignored otherwise. [conditional_after]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : For `type="survival"`, optionally compute conditional survival $P(T > t \mid T > c) = S(t) / S(c)$. Also used with `type="mean"` and `type="mean_remaining"` to condition on surviving past a landmark time $t_0$. Scalar (same conditioning time for all subjects) or array-like (one per subject). Default `None` (unconditional). [ci]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [bool]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[False]{.va}]{.doc-parameter-default} : If `True` (survival only), include confidence intervals (`_lower` and `_upper` columns per subject). Default is `False`. Not supported with `conditional_after`. [conf_type]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [["log-log"]{.st}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Confidence interval transform (used only if `ci=True` and `type="survival"`): - `"log-log"` (default): Log-log transform. Bounds respect the constraint that survival $S(t) \in (0, 1)$. Recommended. - `"plain"`: Wald bounds without transform. Simple but may produce invalid bounds (survival < 0 or > 1). [format]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Output format for the returned frame (`type="quantile"` or `"survival"`): `None` (default), `"pandas"`, `"polars"`, or `"pyarrow"`. When `None`, a backend is auto-detected (Polars, then Pandas, then PyArrow). Ignored for `type="lp"`, `"mean"`, `"mean_remaining"`, and `"rmst"` (which always return arrays). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ndarray or DataFrame]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : If `type="lp"`: an array of shape (n_subjects,) containing log-time locations. If `type="quantile"`: a DataFrame with columns `p` (failure probabilities) and `subject_1`, `subject_2`, etc. containing survival times at each p. If `type="survival"`: a DataFrame with columns `time` (query times) and `subject_1`, `subject_2`, etc. containing survival probabilities at each time, optionally with `_lower` and `_upper` columns for confidence intervals. If `type="mean"`: an array of shape (n_subjects,) with $E[T]$ or $E[T \mid T > t_0]$. If `type="mean_remaining"`: an array of shape (n_subjects,) with $E[T - t_0 \mid T > t_0]$. If `type="rmst"`: an array of shape (n_subjects,) with $E[\min(T, \tau)]$. ::: #### Details {.doc-details} The AFT model assumes $\log(T) = X\beta + \sigma\varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ follows a parametric error distribution (Weibull, lognormal, etc.). Predictions are made by evaluating the CDF/survival function of this distribution at covariate-adjusted locations. All predictions respect the fitted distribution and scale parameter. Predictions assume the model is well-specified. For flexible models, consider parametric bootstrap to quantify uncertainty. #### Examples {.doc-examples} Fit a Weibull AFT model on the bundled `lung` dataset, then predict the linear predictor (log-time location) for the first two subjects: ::: {#33d17579 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) aft = gw.AFT("weibull").fit(y, lung[["age", "sex"]]) aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="lp") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` array([5.749914 , 5.82345845]) ``` ::: ::: Predicted survival-time quantiles for the first two subjects at the lower quartile, median, and upper quartile (a table, so pass `format=`): ::: {#1f8dfba4 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="quantile", p=[0.25, 0.5, 0.75], format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
shape: (3, 3)
psubject_1subject_2
f64f64f64
0.25122.785883132.156457
0.5238.303358256.489806
0.75401.903889432.575736
``` ::: ::: Read survival probabilities off the fitted curves at chosen times. Here are the estimates at 180 and 365 days for those same two subjects: ::: {#4a122b92 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="survival", times=[180, 365], format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
shape: (2, 3)
timesubject_1subject_2
f64f64f64
180.00.6201630.648317
365.00.2952110.330653
``` ::: ::: Predict conditional survival given already having survived to 100 days: ::: {#896c8a96 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="survival", times=[180, 365], conditional_after=100, format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ```{=html}
shape: (2, 3)
timesubject_1subject_2
f64f64f64
180.00.7720930.790875
365.00.3675330.403359
``` ::: ::: Add confidence intervals with `ci=True`: ::: {#04a313e2 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="survival", times=[180, 365], ci=True, format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ```{=html}
shape: (2, 7)
timesubject_1subject_1_lowersubject_1_uppersubject_2subject_2_lowersubject_2_upper
f64f64f64f64f64f64f64
180.00.6201630.5359480.6935180.6483170.5877870.70227
365.00.2952110.2033650.3927510.3306530.2574350.405532
``` ::: ::: Unconditional mean survival time per subject: ::: {#979a958a .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="mean") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=9} ``` array([289.02746597, 311.08499373]) ``` ::: ::: Expected remaining lifetime given the subject has already survived 100 days: ::: {#81aec573 .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="mean_remaining", conditional_after=100) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=10} ``` array([246.28738556, 267.29476956]) ``` ::: ::: Restricted mean survival time up to 365 days: ::: {#886d29f0 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} aft.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]][:2], type="rmst", tau=365) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ``` array([230.17701765, 239.03377795]) ``` ::: ::: ### [to_frame()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Return the coefficient table as a DataFrame. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_parametric.py#L776-L821){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python to_frame(*, format=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Exports one row per term, including the intercept, with coefficient estimates, standard errors, Wald statistics, p-values, and confidence limits. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [format]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Output format: `None` (default), `"pandas"`, `"polars"`, or `"pyarrow"`. When `None`, a backend is auto-detected (Polars, then Pandas, then PyArrow). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [pandas.DataFrame, polars.DataFrame, or pyarrow.Table]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A tidy table with columns `term`, `estimate`, `std_error`, `statistic`, `p_value`, `conf_low`, and `conf_high`. ::: #### Raises {.doc-raises} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ImportError]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : If the requested (or, when auto-detecting, any) DataFrame library is not installed. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Fit a Weibull AFT model on the bundled `lung` dataset, then export its coefficient table as a Polars frame: ::: {#c56bd07e .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) aft = gw.AFT("weibull").fit(y, lung[["age", "sex"]]) aft.to_frame(format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ```{=html}
